1

Efficient SAR Vessel Detection for FPGA-Based On-Satellite Sensing

2 3 Rapid analysis of satellite imagery within minutes-to-hours of acquisition is increasingly vital for many remote sensing applications, and is an essential component for developing next-generation autonomous and distributed satellite systems. On-satellite machine learning (ML) has the potential for such rapid analysis, by overcoming latency associated with intermittent satellite connectivity to ground stations or relay satellites, but state-of-the-art models are often too large or power-hungry for on-board deployment. 4 5 Vessel detection using Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) is a critical time-sensitive application in maritime security that exemplifies this challenge. SAR vessel detection has previously been demonstrated only by ML models that either are too large for satellite deployment, have not been developed for sufficiently low-power hardware, or have only been tested on small SAR datasets that do not sufficiently represent the difficulty of the real-world task. 6 7 Here we systematically explore a suite of architectural adaptations to develop a novel YOLOv8 architecture optimized for this task and FPGA-based processing. We deploy our model on a Kria KV260 MPSoC, and show it can analyze a ~700 megapixel SAR image in less than a minute, within common satellite power constraints (<10W). Our model has detection and classification performance only ~2\% and 3\% lower than values from state-of-the-art GPU-based models on the largest and most diverse open SAR vessel dataset, xView3-SAR, despite being ~50 and ~2500 times more computationally efficient. This work represents a key contribution towards on-satellite ML for time-critical SAR analysis, and more autonomous, scalable satellites. 8 9 ![Multi Panel](./assets/multi_panel_v2.jpg) 10 SAR vessel detection using Sentinel-1 imagery from the xView3-SAR dataset. The images show ground truth vessel locations and the multi-channel model inputs (VV, VH, and bathymetry). 11 12 ## Repository Structure 13 This repository is structured to facilitate the development, training, and deployment of the YOLOv8 model for SAR vessel detection. The main components are: 14 - **`framework/vitis_ai/`**: Contains the Vitis AI 3.5 patch to for the additional features and performance optimizations used in this work. 15 - **`platform/kv260/`**: Contains the hardware design files, scripts and instructions to build the firmware for Vitis AI 3.5 on the Kria KV260 MPSoC. This includes Vivado design files, DPU configurations, and scripts for building the hardware platform. 16 - **`platform/post_processing/`**: Contains the Vitis HLS implementation of the YOLOv8 bounding box decoding post-processing step, optimized for PL execution on the KV260 MPSoC (application, kernel, host). 17 - **`software/training/`**: Contains the training of YOLOv8 models on the xView3-SAR dataset, including custom modifications to the YOLOv8 framework. 18 - **`software/quantization/`**: Contains the Vitis AI details for post-training quantization and quantization aware training of the YOLOv8 model. 19 - **`software/inference_app/`**: Contains the code for running inference on the KV260 MPSoC, including the necessary scripts and configurations for deploying the trained model. 20 21 22 ![xView3 Comparison](./assets/xview3_models.jpg) 23 F1-Score vs Model Size comparison. 24 25 ## Citation 26 27 If you use this repository or its results in your research, please cite: 28 29 > Colin Laganier, Liam Fletcher, Elim Kwan, Richard Walters, and Victoria Nockles. 2025. *Efficient SAR Vessel Detection for FPGA-Based On-Satellite Sensing.* In *The Tenth ACM/IEEE Symposium on Edge Computing (SEC ’25)*, December 3–6, 2025, Arlington, VA, USA. ACM, New York, NY, USA, 17 pages. [https://doi.org/10.1145/3769102.3772713](https://doi.org/10.1145/3769102.3772713) 30 31 32 ## Acknowledgment 33 This work was supported by the Turing's Defence and Security programme through a partnership with Dstl. This research used the Baskerville Tier 2 HPC; funded by EPSRC & UKRI (EP/T022221/1, EP/W032244/1) and operated by Advanced Research Computing, U. Birmingham. Copernicus Sentinel-1 SAR data are provided courtesy of ESA. 34 35

1 # KV260 Vitis AI Custom Build 2 3 This document provides a comprehensive guide for setting up the hardware design and building a custom PetaLinux image for Vitis AI 3.5 on the Kria KV260 SOM. The process covers the Vivado hardware flow, PetaLinux software build, firmware creation, and final deployment on the board. 4 5 ## Prerequisites 6 7 Before you begin, ensure you have the following software installed and configured: 8 9 * **Vivado ML Edition 2023.2** 10 * **PetaLinux 2023.2** 11 * The **VAI-3.5-ZUP-DPU-TRD** project script (originally for ZU+ DPU) 12 13 ## Workflow Overview 14 15 The process is divided into four main stages: 16 17 1. **Hardware Design:** Creating a custom hardware platform with a DPU using Vivado. 18 2. **Software Build:** Building a PetaLinux image with the necessary drivers and configurations. 19 3. **Firmware Preparation:** Generating the required firmware files for the accelerator. 20 4. **Deployment & Verification:** Flashing the SD card, loading the firmware onto the KV260, and verifying the DPU operation. 21 22 ----- 23 24 ## 1\. Hardware Setup (Vivado) 25 26 This section details the creation of the Vivado hardware platform. The goal is to adapt a reference design for the KV260 and export the hardware definition file (`.xsa`). 27 28 29 ![Vivado KV260 DPU TRD](../../assets/Vivado_KV260_DPU_TRD.png) 30 Vivado Design for KV260 DPU 31 32 33 We provide a modified `.tcl` script to build the Vivado design based on our configuration. 34 35 ```bash 36 vivado -mode batch -source main.tcl 37 ``` 38
39 FPGA Resource Utilization 40 41 The following table summarizes the FPGA resource utilization for this DPU design after implementation in Vivado. 42 43 | Site Type | Used | Fixed | Prohibited | Available | Util% | 44 | :--- | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | 45 | **CLB LUTs**\* | 62104 | 0 | 0 | 117120 | 53.03 | 46 | - LUT as Logic | 54474 | 0 | 0 | 117120 | 46.51 | 47 | - LUT as Memory | 7630 | 0 | 0 | 57600 | 13.25 | 48 | -- LUT as Distributed RAM | 2948 | 0 | | | | 49 | -- LUT as Shift Register | 4682 | 0 | | | | 50 | **CLB Registers** | 106965 | 0 | 0 | 234240 | 45.66 | 51 | - Register as Flip Flop | 106961 | 0 | 0 | 234240 | 45.66 | 52 | - Register as Latch | 4 | 0 | 0 | 234240 | \<0.01 | 53 | **CARRY8** | 1969 | 0 | 0 | 14640 | 13.45 | 54 | **F7 Muxes** | 3267 | 0 | 0 | 58560 | 5.58 | 55 | **F8 Muxes** | 363 | 0 | 0 | 29280 | 1.24 | 56 | **F9 Muxes** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 14640 | 0.00 | 57 | **Block RAM Tile** | 109 | 0 | 0 | 144 | 75.69 | 58 | - RAMB36/FIFO\* | 102 | 0 | 0 | 144 | 70.83 | 59 | -- RAMB36E2 only | 102 | | | | | 60 | - RAMB18 | 14 | 0 | 0 | 288 | 4.86 | 61 | -- RAMB18E2 only | 14 | | | | | 62 | **URAM** | 40 | 0 | 0 | 64 | 62.50 | 63 64
65 66 To create a Vivado design with the DPU 4.1 ([AMD download link](https://www.xilinx.com/bin/public/openDownload?filename=DPUCZDX8G_VAI_v3.0.tar.gz)) for any other boards, an alternative method is detailed below. 67 68
69 Manual Design Steps 70 71 1. **Generate Reference Design:** Use the `VAI-3.5-ZUP-DPU-TRD` script to generate a reference design. While the script may target a different board (e.g., ZCU104), it provides a baseline DPU configuration. 72 73 ```bash 74 vivado -mode batch -source main.tcl -tclargs zcu104 75 ``` 76 77 2. **Recreate for KV260:** Manually recreate the design topology from the generated project in a new Vivado 2023.2 project, but target the Kria KV260 SOM. 78 79 > **Note:** This repository includes a pre-generated TCL file (`/hw/project.tcl`) which can be used to script the creation of the Vivado project. 80 81 3. **Select Board Part:** When creating the project, select the appropriate board part for your hardware. The `xilinx-kv260-starterkit-2023.2` platform files use the following part: 82 83 * `xilinx.com:kv260_som_som240_1_connector_kv260_carrier_som240_1_connector:part0:1.4` 84 85 4. **Add Design Constraints:** A Xilinx Design Constraints (`.xdc`) file is required to map the design to the physical pins of the device. The `xilinx-kv260-starterkit-2023.2` platform provides a `default.xdc` file with essential constraints. 86 87 ```xdc 88 # Copyright (C) 2022, Xilinx, Inc. 89 # Copyright (C) 2022, Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. 90 # SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0 91 92 set_property BITSTREAM.GENERAL.COMPRESS TRUE [current_design] 93 94 #Fan Speed Enable 95 set_property PACKAGE_PIN A12 [get_ports {fan_en_b}] 96 set_property IOSTANDARD LVCMOS33 [get_ports {fan_en_b}] 97 set_property SLEW SLOW [get_ports {fan_en_b}] 98 set_property DRIVE 4 [get_ports {fan_en_b}] 99 ``` 100 101 5. **Generate Hardware File:** After validating the design, generate the bitstream and export the **Hardware Platform (`.xsa` file)**. This file is the crucial handoff from the hardware team to the software team. 102
103 104 ## 2\. Software Build (PetaLinux) 105 106 This section covers the creation of the custom Linux image using PetaLinux. 107 108 ### Steps 109 110 1. **Source PetaLinux:** Open a terminal and source the PetaLinux settings script. 111 112 ```bash 113 source ~/petalinux/2023.2/settings.sh 114 # Verify the environment variable is set 115 echo $PETALINUX 116 ``` 117 118 2. **Build BSP:** Modify the provided `helper_build_bsp.sh` script to target the KV260 and run it. The script will automate the PetaLinux project creation and configuration process. 119 120 ```bash 121 bash helper_build_bsp.sh 122 ``` 123 124 3. **Configure Kernel:** In the PetaLinux project, open the kernel configuration menu and enable the DPU driver. 125 126 ```bash 127 # From within the PetaLinux project directory 128 petalinux-config -c kernel 129 ``` 130 131 Navigate to and enable the following option: 132 `Device Drivers --> Misc devices --> <*> Xilinx Deep learning Processing Unit (DPU) Driver` 133 134 4. **Build the Image:** Build the PetaLinux image. 135 136 ```bash 137 petalinux-build 138 ``` 139 140 ### Troubleshooting Build Errors 141 142 * **`do_image_cpio` Error:** If the `rootfs` is larger than 2GB, the build may fail during the `do_image_cpio` task because `INITRAMFS` cannot be used. According to PetaLinux documentation, adding the following lines to `/project-spec/meta-user/conf/petalinuxbsp.conf` can resolve this by preventing `cpio` images from being generated. 143 144 ``` 145 IMAGE_FSTYPES:remove = "cpio cpio.gz cpio.bz2 cpio.xz cpio.lzma cpio.lz4 cpio.gz.u-boot" 146 IMAGE_FSTYPES_DEBUGFS:remove = "cpio cpio.gz cpio.bz2 cpio.xz cpio.lzma cpio.lz4 cpio.gz.u-boot" 147 ``` 148 149 * **Fetcher/Sysroot Errors:** Adding the lines above may lead to `do_populate_sysroot` or `lic_setscene` errors for various packages (`lighttpd`, `mdadm`, `python3-*`, etc.). 150 151 * **Resolution:** In our experience, cleaning and rerunning the `petalinux-build` command multiple times eventually resulted in a successful build. The root cause of the initial failures is unclear. 152 153 ## 3\. SD Card and Firmware Preparation 154 155 After a successful build, you must prepare the SD card and the custom accelerator firmware files. 156 157 ### 3.1. Flashing the SD Card 158 159 The generated SD card image is located in the `/images/linux/` directory. 160 161 1. **Flash Image:** Use a tool like Balena Etcher or `dd` to flash the `petalinux-sdimage.wic` file onto your microSD card. 162 163 2. **Alternative Partitioning:** For more control, you can manually partition the SD card and copy the files. 164 165 * **Partition 1 (boot):** Format as `FAT32`. Copy `BOOT.BIN`, `image.ub`, and `boot.scr`. 166 * **Partition 2 (root):** Format as `ext4`. Extract the `rootfs.tar.gz` archive to this partition. 167 * For detailed instructions, see the [PetaLinux Tools Reference Guide (UG1144)](https://docs.amd.com/r/en-US/ug1144-petalinux-tools-reference-guide/PetaLinux-Configuration-and-Build-System-Image). 168 169 ### 3.2. Creating Accelerator Firmware 170 171 The accelerator requires three files: the bitstream, a device tree overlay, and a shell metadata file. 172 173 1. **Generate Device Tree Overlay (DTBO):** 174 175 * Use the **XSCT** terminal (included with Vitis/Vivado/PetaLinux) to create a Device Tree Source Include (`.dtsi`) file from your exported hardware file (`.xsa`). 176 ```bash 177 # In XSCT terminal 178 createdts -hw /path/to/your/project.xsa -zocl -platform-name KV260 -git-branch xlnx_rel_v2023.2 -overlay -compile -out /path/to/output_dtsi_dir/ 179 ``` 180 * The Kria SOM requires a binary device tree format (`.dtbo`). Use the **Device Tree Compiler (`dtc`)** to convert the generated `.dtsi` file. 181 ```bash 182 # Install dtc if you haven't already 183 sudo apt install dtc 184 # Convert the file 185 dtc -@ -O dtb -o ./kv260.dtbo /path/to/output_dtsi_dir/KV260/psu_cortexa53_0/device_tree_domain/bsp/pl.dtsi 186 ``` 187 188 2. **Prepare Bitstream File:** 189 190 * Locate the bitstream file generated by Vivado (e.g., `project_1.bit`). 191 * Copy it and rename the copy to `project_1.bit.bin`. This specific naming is expected by the firmware loading utility. 192 193 3. **Create shell.json:** 194 195 * This file informs the system about the nature of the shell. Create it with the following content. 196 ```bash 197 echo '{ "shell_type" : "XRT_FLAT", "num_slots": "1" }' > shell.json 198 ``` 199 200 ## 4\. Deployment and Verification 201 202 The final step is to copy the firmware to the booted device and load the accelerator. 203 204 1. **Boot the KV260:** Insert the flashed SD card, connect the Ethernet cable, and power on the board. 205 206 2. **Connect to the Board:** 207 208 * Use the serial console to find the IP address or set a static one (e.g., `ifconfig eth0 192.168.1.99`). The default login credentials are `root:root`. 209 * Copy the three firmware files (`kv260.dtbo`, `project_1.bit.bin`, `shell.json`) to the board using `scp`. 210 ```bash 211 scp kv260.dtbo project_1.bit.bin shell.json root@:/home/root/ 212 ``` 213 214 3. **Load the Firmware (on KV260):** 215 216 * SSH into the board. 217 * Create a directory for your custom firmware. The directory name will be the name of your application. 218 ```bash 219 ssh root@ 220 mkdir -p /lib/firmware/xilinx/kv260-dpu-trd 221 ``` 222 * Move the firmware files into this new directory. 223 ```bash 224 mv /home/root/{kv260.dtbo,project_1.bit.bin,shell.json} /lib/firmware/xilinx/kv260-dpu-trd/ 225 ``` 226 * Unload any currently active accelerator. 227 ```bash 228 xmutil unloadapp 229 ``` 230 * Load your new custom application. 231 ```bash 232 xmutil loadapp kv260-dpu-trd 233 # Expected output: Accelerator loaded to slot 0 234 ``` 235 236 4. **Verify DPU Integration:** 237 238 * Run `xdputil query` to check the status of the DPU. If successful, you should see output similar to the following, confirming the DPU architecture, frequency, and Vitis AI versions. 239 ```json 240 $ xdputil query 241 { 242 "DPU IP Spec":{ 243 "DPU Core Count":1, 244 "IP version":"v4.1.0", 245 "enable softmax":"True" 246 }, 247 "VAI Version":{ 248 "libvart-runner.so":"Xilinx vart-runner Version: 3.5.0-b7953a2a9f60e23efdfced5c186328dd1449665c 2024-10-02-13:24:00 ", 249 "libvitis_ai_library-dpu_task.so":"Advanced Micro Devices vitis_ai_library dpu_task Version: 3.5.0-b7953a2a9f60e23efdfced5c186328dd1449665c 2023-06-29 03:20:28 [UTC] ", 250 "libxir.so":"Xilinx xir Version: xir-b7953a2a9f60e23efdfced5c186328dd1449665c 2024-10-02-13:20:31", 251 "target_factory":"target-factory.3.5.0 b7953a2a9f60e23efdfced5c186328dd1449665c" 252 }, 253 "kernels":[ 254 { 255 "DPU Arch":"DPUCZDX8G_ISA1_B4096", 256 "DPU Frequency (MHz)":325, 257 "XRT Frequency (MHz)":100, 258 "cu_idx":0, 259 "fingerprint":"0x101000056010407", 260 "is_vivado_flow":true, 261 "name":"DPU Core 0" 262 } 263 ] 264 } 265 ``` 266 267 You have now successfully built and deployed a custom Vitis AI 3.5 design on the KV260.

# KV260 Vitis AI Custom Build This document provides a comprehensive guide for setting up the hardware design and building a custom PetaLinux image for Vitis AI 3.5 on the Kria KV260 SOM. The process covers the Vivado hardware flow, PetaLinux software build, firmware creation, and final deployment on the board. ## Prerequisites Before you begin, ensure you have the following software installed and configured: * **Vivado ML Edition 2023.2** * **PetaLinux 2023.2** * The **VAI-3.5-ZUP-DPU-TRD** project script (originally for ZU+ DPU) ## Workflow Overview The process is divided into four main stages: 1. **Hardware Design:** Creating a custom hardware platform with a DPU using Vivado. 2. **Software Build:** Building a PetaLinux image with the necessary drivers and configurations. 3. **Firmware Preparation:** Generating the required firmware files for the accelerator. 4. **Deployment & Verification:** Flashing the SD card, loading the firmware onto the KV260, and verifying the DPU operation. ----- ## 1\. Hardware Setup (Vivado) This section details the creation of the Vivado hardware platform. The goal is to adapt a reference design for the KV260 and export the hardware definition file (`.xsa`). ![Vivado KV260 DPU TRD](../../assets/Vivado_KV260_DPU_TRD.png) Vivado Design for KV260 DPU We provide a modified `.tcl` script to build the Vivado design based on our configuration. ```bash vivado -mode batch -source main.tcl ```
FPGA Resource Utilization The following table summarizes the FPGA resource utilization for this DPU design after implementation in Vivado. | Site Type | Used | Fixed | Prohibited | Available | Util% | | :--- | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | | **CLB LUTs**\* | 62104 | 0 | 0 | 117120 | 53.03 | | - LUT as Logic | 54474 | 0 | 0 | 117120 | 46.51 | | - LUT as Memory | 7630 | 0 | 0 | 57600 | 13.25 | | -- LUT as Distributed RAM | 2948 | 0 | | | | | -- LUT as Shift Register | 4682 | 0 | | | | | **CLB Registers** | 106965 | 0 | 0 | 234240 | 45.66 | | - Register as Flip Flop | 106961 | 0 | 0 | 234240 | 45.66 | | - Register as Latch | 4 | 0 | 0 | 234240 | \<0.01 | | **CARRY8** | 1969 | 0 | 0 | 14640 | 13.45 | | **F7 Muxes** | 3267 | 0 | 0 | 58560 | 5.58 | | **F8 Muxes** | 363 | 0 | 0 | 29280 | 1.24 | | **F9 Muxes** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 14640 | 0.00 | | **Block RAM Tile** | 109 | 0 | 0 | 144 | 75.69 | | - RAMB36/FIFO\* | 102 | 0 | 0 | 144 | 70.83 | | -- RAMB36E2 only | 102 | | | | | | - RAMB18 | 14 | 0 | 0 | 288 | 4.86 | | -- RAMB18E2 only | 14 | | | | | | **URAM** | 40 | 0 | 0 | 64 | 62.50 |
To create a Vivado design with the DPU 4.1 ([AMD download link](https://www.xilinx.com/bin/public/openDownload?filename=DPUCZDX8G_VAI_v3.0.tar.gz)) for any other boards, an alternative method is detailed below.
Manual Design Steps 1. **Generate Reference Design:** Use the `VAI-3.5-ZUP-DPU-TRD` script to generate a reference design. While the script may target a different board (e.g., ZCU104), it provides a baseline DPU configuration. ```bash vivado -mode batch -source main.tcl -tclargs zcu104 ``` 2. **Recreate for KV260:** Manually recreate the design topology from the generated project in a new Vivado 2023.2 project, but target the Kria KV260 SOM. > **Note:** This repository includes a pre-generated TCL file (`/hw/project.tcl`) which can be used to script the creation of the Vivado project. 3. **Select Board Part:** When creating the project, select the appropriate board part for your hardware. The `xilinx-kv260-starterkit-2023.2` platform files use the following part: * `xilinx.com:kv260_som_som240_1_connector_kv260_carrier_som240_1_connector:part0:1.4` 4. **Add Design Constraints:** A Xilinx Design Constraints (`.xdc`) file is required to map the design to the physical pins of the device. The `xilinx-kv260-starterkit-2023.2` platform provides a `default.xdc` file with essential constraints. ```xdc # Copyright (C) 2022, Xilinx, Inc. # Copyright (C) 2022, Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. # SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0 set_property BITSTREAM.GENERAL.COMPRESS TRUE [current_design] #Fan Speed Enable set_property PACKAGE_PIN A12 [get_ports {fan_en_b}] set_property IOSTANDARD LVCMOS33 [get_ports {fan_en_b}] set_property SLEW SLOW [get_ports {fan_en_b}] set_property DRIVE 4 [get_ports {fan_en_b}] ``` 5. **Generate Hardware File:** After validating the design, generate the bitstream and export the **Hardware Platform (`.xsa` file)**. This file is the crucial handoff from the hardware team to the software team.
## 2\. Software Build (PetaLinux) This section covers the creation of the custom Linux image using PetaLinux. ### Steps 1. **Source PetaLinux:** Open a terminal and source the PetaLinux settings script. ```bash source ~/petalinux/2023.2/settings.sh # Verify the environment variable is set echo $PETALINUX ``` 2. **Build BSP:** Modify the provided `helper_build_bsp.sh` script to target the KV260 and run it. The script will automate the PetaLinux project creation and configuration process. ```bash bash helper_build_bsp.sh ``` 3. **Configure Kernel:** In the PetaLinux project, open the kernel configuration menu and enable the DPU driver. ```bash # From within the PetaLinux project directory petalinux-config -c kernel ``` Navigate to and enable the following option: `Device Drivers --> Misc devices --> <*> Xilinx Deep learning Processing Unit (DPU) Driver` 4. **Build the Image:** Build the PetaLinux image. ```bash petalinux-build ``` ### Troubleshooting Build Errors * **`do_image_cpio` Error:** If the `rootfs` is larger than 2GB, the build may fail during the `do_image_cpio` task because `INITRAMFS` cannot be used. According to PetaLinux documentation, adding the following lines to `/project-spec/meta-user/conf/petalinuxbsp.conf` can resolve this by preventing `cpio` images from being generated. ``` IMAGE_FSTYPES:remove = "cpio cpio.gz cpio.bz2 cpio.xz cpio.lzma cpio.lz4 cpio.gz.u-boot" IMAGE_FSTYPES_DEBUGFS:remove = "cpio cpio.gz cpio.bz2 cpio.xz cpio.lzma cpio.lz4 cpio.gz.u-boot" ``` * **Fetcher/Sysroot Errors:** Adding the lines above may lead to `do_populate_sysroot` or `lic_setscene` errors for various packages (`lighttpd`, `mdadm`, `python3-*`, etc.). * **Resolution:** In our experience, cleaning and rerunning the `petalinux-build` command multiple times eventually resulted in a successful build. The root cause of the initial failures is unclear. ## 3\. SD Card and Firmware Preparation After a successful build, you must prepare the SD card and the custom accelerator firmware files. ### 3.1. Flashing the SD Card The generated SD card image is located in the `/images/linux/` directory. 1. **Flash Image:** Use a tool like Balena Etcher or `dd` to flash the `petalinux-sdimage.wic` file onto your microSD card. 2. **Alternative Partitioning:** For more control, you can manually partition the SD card and copy the files. * **Partition 1 (boot):** Format as `FAT32`. Copy `BOOT.BIN`, `image.ub`, and `boot.scr`. * **Partition 2 (root):** Format as `ext4`. Extract the `rootfs.tar.gz` archive to this partition. * For detailed instructions, see the [PetaLinux Tools Reference Guide (UG1144)](https://docs.amd.com/r/en-US/ug1144-petalinux-tools-reference-guide/PetaLinux-Configuration-and-Build-System-Image). ### 3.2. Creating Accelerator Firmware The accelerator requires three files: the bitstream, a device tree overlay, and a shell metadata file. 1. **Generate Device Tree Overlay (DTBO):** * Use the **XSCT** terminal (included with Vitis/Vivado/PetaLinux) to create a Device Tree Source Include (`.dtsi`) file from your exported hardware file (`.xsa`). ```bash # In XSCT terminal createdts -hw /path/to/your/project.xsa -zocl -platform-name KV260 -git-branch xlnx_rel_v2023.2 -overlay -compile -out /path/to/output_dtsi_dir/ ``` * The Kria SOM requires a binary device tree format (`.dtbo`). Use the **Device Tree Compiler (`dtc`)** to convert the generated `.dtsi` file. ```bash # Install dtc if you haven't already sudo apt install dtc # Convert the file dtc -@ -O dtb -o ./kv260.dtbo /path/to/output_dtsi_dir/KV260/psu_cortexa53_0/device_tree_domain/bsp/pl.dtsi ``` 2. **Prepare Bitstream File:** * Locate the bitstream file generated by Vivado (e.g., `project_1.bit`). * Copy it and rename the copy to `project_1.bit.bin`. This specific naming is expected by the firmware loading utility. 3. **Create shell.json:** * This file informs the system about the nature of the shell. Create it with the following content. ```bash echo '{ "shell_type" : "XRT_FLAT", "num_slots": "1" }' > shell.json ``` ## 4\. Deployment and Verification The final step is to copy the firmware to the booted device and load the accelerator. 1. **Boot the KV260:** Insert the flashed SD card, connect the Ethernet cable, and power on the board. 2. **Connect to the Board:** * Use the serial console to find the IP address or set a static one (e.g., `ifconfig eth0 192.168.1.99`). The default login credentials are `root:root`. * Copy the three firmware files (`kv260.dtbo`, `project_1.bit.bin`, `shell.json`) to the board using `scp`. ```bash scp kv260.dtbo project_1.bit.bin shell.json root@:/home/root/ ``` 3. **Load the Firmware (on KV260):** * SSH into the board. * Create a directory for your custom firmware. The directory name will be the name of your application. ```bash ssh root@ mkdir -p /lib/firmware/xilinx/kv260-dpu-trd ``` * Move the firmware files into this new directory. ```bash mv /home/root/{kv260.dtbo,project_1.bit.bin,shell.json} /lib/firmware/xilinx/kv260-dpu-trd/ ``` * Unload any currently active accelerator. ```bash xmutil unloadapp ``` * Load your new custom application. ```bash xmutil loadapp kv260-dpu-trd # Expected output: Accelerator loaded to slot 0 ``` 4. **Verify DPU Integration:** * Run `xdputil query` to check the status of the DPU. If successful, you should see output similar to the following, confirming the DPU architecture, frequency, and Vitis AI versions. ```json $ xdputil query { "DPU IP Spec":{ "DPU Core Count":1, "IP version":"v4.1.0", "enable softmax":"True" }, "VAI Version":{ "libvart-runner.so":"Xilinx vart-runner Version: 3.5.0-b7953a2a9f60e23efdfced5c186328dd1449665c 2024-10-02-13:24:00 ", "libvitis_ai_library-dpu_task.so":"Advanced Micro Devices vitis_ai_library dpu_task Version: 3.5.0-b7953a2a9f60e23efdfced5c186328dd1449665c 2023-06-29 03:20:28 [UTC] ", "libxir.so":"Xilinx xir Version: xir-b7953a2a9f60e23efdfced5c186328dd1449665c 2024-10-02-13:20:31", "target_factory":"target-factory.3.5.0 b7953a2a9f60e23efdfced5c186328dd1449665c" }, "kernels":[ { "DPU Arch":"DPUCZDX8G_ISA1_B4096", "DPU Frequency (MHz)":325, "XRT Frequency (MHz)":100, "cu_idx":0, "fingerprint":"0x101000056010407", "is_vivado_flow":true, "name":"DPU Core 0" } ] } ``` You have now successfully built and deployed a custom Vitis AI 3.5 design on the KV260.

# Post-Processing Decoding HLS Kernel

This repository contains a Vitis HLS (High-Level Synthesis) kernel implementation for a YOLOv8 bounding box decoding kernel, and the supporting host and packaging files required to synthesize, simulate, and deploy it onto an FPGA-based platform (AMD/Xilinx KV260 MPSoC).

The decoding step transforms the raw output feature maps from the YOLOv8 heads into interpretable bounding box coordinates, confidence scores, and class probabilities. This kernel is designed to accelerate the decoding process on FPGA hardware, enabling real-time object detection applications.

## Repository Structure

- **`decode_common/`** — shared headers used by host and kernel.
- **`decode_host/`** — host-side application and CMake build for running the kernel host
- **`decode_krnl/`** — HLS kernel sources, testbench, and HLS config.
- **`decodeapp/`** — higher-level application packaging and hardware link artifacts.
- **`kv260_hardware_platform/`** — included hardware platform and bitstream, XSA for KV260 target.
- **`reports/`** — Vitis HLS reports
- **`dtg_output/`** — device tree overlays and shell description

## Decoding Platform Creation and Deployment
The decoding kernel creation process follows the Vitis Platform Creation Tutorial, for additional details see the [documentation](https://github.com/Xilinx/Vitis-Tutorials/tree/2023.1/Vitis_Platform_Creation/Design_Tutorials/01-Edge-KV260). To customize or recreate the kernel, ensure Vitis and Vivado 2023.1 is installed as well as CMake and a C++ toolchain.

1. **Vivado Platform Hardware Design:** The repository includes an example KV260 platform in `kv260_hardware_platform/` (XSA and .bit). Use the matching Vitis/Vivado 2023.1 tool version that was used to create those files for a reproducible hardware flow.

    To recreate the platform, create a new Vivado project with the KV260 board selected, and follow the steps in the Vitis Platform Creation tutorial to add the necessary IP blocks, configure the processing system, and generate the hardware platform.
    Once the block design is complete and the clocks and resets are properly configured, export the hardware including the bitstream to generate the XSA file.

2. **Vitis Platform Creation:** With the hardware platform XSA file generated from Vivado, follow the Vitis Platform Creation tutorial to set up a new Vitis platform project. This can be done from the Xilinx ZynqMP common image or from the  KV260 Starter Kit BSP. Using the XSA file generate the device tree overlay (provided in `dtg_output/`) and shell description.

    With these components in place, you can proceed to install the PetaLinux SDK from the downloaded Xilinx image and create a Vitis platform using the Vitis Unified IDE.

3. **Build the Kernel and Application:** In your created Vitis platform, you can now import the HLS kernel from `decode_krnl/` and build it targeting the hardware platform. After successfully synthesizing and implementing the kernel, package it into an XCLBIN file. The kernel can be evaluated using the provided testbench in `decode_krnl/` to ensure correctness before deployment.

    Next, import the host application from `decode_host/` into your Vitis workspace, link it against the generated XCLBIN, and build the host application for deployment on the KV260 board.
4. **Deployment and Platform Testing:** Once the host application is built, you can deploy it onto the KV260 board for testing. This involves copying the overlay files to the board, and loading the hardware with `xmutil`. 

    The `decodeapp/` directory contains the script to call the kernel on the target hardware, along with the required packaging files to facilitate deployment. The script can then be executed onboard to verify the functionality of the decoding kernel, either with pre-computed feature map outputs from the YOLOv8m model or letting the script generate random inputs for testing.

For full codebase and more information on the YOLOv8 framework, see the [Ultralytics YOLOv8 repository](http://github.com/ultralytics/ultralytics) and [Ultralytics YOLOv8 documentation](https://docs.ultralytics.com/). 4 5 ![YOLOv8 Diagram](../../assets/yolov8_diagram.jpg) 6 Diagram of YOLOv8 architecture. (a) feature extraction backbone, (b) multi-scale feature fusion neck, (c) detection head, (d) simplified end-to-end detection pipeline 7 8 ## Requirements 9 Install all the packages from environment.yml file using conda: 10 11 ```bash 12 conda env create -f environment.yml 13 ``` 14 15 ## Installation 16 17 Install this package as an editable package using `pip` with the following command: 18 ```bash 19 pip install -e . 20 ``` 21 >[!WARNING] 22 >Ensure that the Python package installation path is included in your system's `PATH` environment variable. This is typically `$HOME/Library/Python//bin` or `/usr/local/bin` for a Mac. 23 24 ## Framework Modifications 25 26 1. **Image Loading:** To read tiff files the `cv2.IMREAD_UNCHANGED` flag must be added to every `cv2.imread` calls. 27 28 2. **Custom Image Normalisation:** Image normalisation must be called in the `get_image_and_label` method of the `BaseDataset` class, after the `load_image` call. In this work we linearly normalise SAR bands in `[-50,20]` range and bathymetry to `[-6000,2000]`: 29 30 ```python 31 def normalize(self, img): 32 min_values = np.array([-6000, -50, -50]) 33 max_values = np.array([2000, 20, 20]) 34 return (np.clip((img - min_values) / (max_values - min_values), 0, 1) * 255).round().astype(np.uint8) 35 ``` 36 37 This allows the augmentation techniques to be used as default with no additional modifications. 38 39 40 3. **Modified IoU Metrics:** PIoU2 (Liu, 2024) metric is used instead of default CIoU. Adding the `compute_piou` method to the `bbox_iou` function in `metrics.py`. 41 42 4. **Validation:** Predictions from each batch are collected, transformed to the global coordinates (using the chip offset values) and stored in a dictionary for evaluation. The following code snippet shows the addition to `update_metrics` method: 43 ```python 44 if self.xview_eval: 45 chip_id = batch["im_file"][si].split("/")[-1].split(".")[0] 46 scene, offset_y, offset_x = self.coords_offset[chip_id] 47 pred_out = predn.cpu() 48 pred_coords = pred_out[:,:2].round().int() 49 is_vessel, is_fishing = decode_cls(pred_out[:, 5]) 50 self.predictions["scene_id"].extend([scene]*npr) 51 self.predictions["detect_scene_column"].extend((offset_x+pred_coords[:, 0]).tolist()) 52 self.predictions["detect_scene_row"].extend((offset_y+pred_coords[:, 1]).tolist()) 53 self.predictions["x"].extend(pred_coords[:, 0]) 54 self.predictions["y"].extend(pred_coords[:, 1]) 55 self.predictions["offset_x"].extend([offset_x]*npr) 56 self.predictions["offset_y"].extend([offset_y]*npr) 57 self.predictions["is_vessel"].extend(is_vessel) 58 self.predictions["is_fishing"].extend(is_fishing) 59 self.predictions["score"].extend(pred_out[:, 4].tolist()) 60 ``` 61 62 5. **xView3 Metrics:** The xView3-SAR competition metrics (Detection, Near-shore Detection, Vessel, Fishing F1-score) and inference post-processing steps are implemented in the `metrics.py` file. This evaluation is called in the `get_stats` method: 63 ```python 64 self.xview3_metrics_out = self.xview3_metrics.process(df) 65 ``` 66 67 ## Usage 68 69 YOLOv8 may be used directly in the Command Line Interface (CLI) with a `yolo` command. Model and dataset configurations are stored in the form of `.yaml` files in `ultralytics/cfg/`. Training 70 71 ```bash 72 # training 73 yolo train model="" data="" imgsz=<800|640> 74 # evaluation 75 yolo val model="" split="test" data="" imgsz=<800|640> 76 # inference 77 yolo predict model="" source="" 78 ``` 79 The default model configuration parameters are located in `ultralytics/cfg/default.yaml` and can be overridden in the CLI for testing and experimentation purposes, see the YOLOv8 [CLI Docs](https://docs.ultralytics.com/usage/cli) for examples. 80 81 ## References 82 C. Liu, K. Wang, Q. Li, F. Zhao, K. Zhao, and H. Ma, “Powerful-IoU: 83 More straightforward and faster bounding box regression loss with a 84 nonmonotonic focusing mechanism,” Neural Networks, vol. 170, pp. 85 276–284, 2024.

4

Please see the [YOLOv7 Vitis AI implementation](https://github.com/Xilinx/Vitis-AI-Copyleft-Model-Zoo/tree/main/yolov7) for a similar implementation, or contact AMD for additional information.
6	
7	## Installation
8	
9	Clone the modified `ultralytics` repo (referred to as `ultralytics-vitis-ai`) in your Vitis AI file system and install the library as an editable package in the running Vitis AI Docker container using `pip` with the following command:
10	```bash
11	cd ultralytics-vitis-ai/
12	conda activate vitis-ai-pytorch
13	pip install -e .
14	```

18	>```bash

20	>```
21	>The YOLOv8 requirements clash with the `pandas` and `matplotlib` libraries included in the Vitis AI conda environment, which can be resolved by running:
22	>```bash
23	>pip install pandas --no-cache-dir --force-reinstall && pip install matplotlib --no-cache-dir --force-reinstall
24	>```
25	
26	## Vitis AI Quantization Steps 
27	
28	This guide outlines the process for quantizing, compiling, and deploying a trained YOLOv8 model to a Xilinx Kria KV260 using the Vitis AI toolchain.
29	
30	
31	### 1\. Model Quantization
32	
33	Model quantization reduces the precision of the model's weights and activations, significantly decreasing its size and improving inference speed on hardware accelerators. You can choose between Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) or Quantization Aware Training (QAT).
34	
35	#### Post-Training Quantization (PTQ)
36	
37	PTQ is a quick method to quantize an already trained model. First, calibrate the model to determine the dynamic range of activations:
38	
39	```bash
40	yolo detect val data="xview3-vitis.yaml" model=<path-to-your-model.pt> nndct_quant=True quant_mode=calib imgsz=800 --nndct_convert_sigmoid_to_hsigmoid --nndct_convert_silu_to_hswish
41	```
42	
43	#### Quantization Aware Training (QAT)
44	
45	QAT can yield better performance by simulating quantization effects during the training process, allowing the model to adapt.
46	
47	```bash
48	yolo detect train cfg="qat.yaml" data="xview3-vitis.yaml" model=<path-to-your-model.pt> imgsz=800 epochs=100 optimizer=SGD momentum=0.9 lr0=0.005 warmup_epochs=0 nndct_quant=True --nndct_convert_sigmoid_to_hsigmoid --nndct_convert_silu_to_hswish
49	```
50	-----
51	
52	### 2\. Model Export and Evaluation
53	
54	Once the model is quantized, you can export it to the `xmodel` format for FPGA deployment and the `ONNX` format for broader compatibility.
55	
56	  * **Export the Quantized Model**:
57	
58	    ```bash
59	    yolo detect val data="xview3-vitis.yaml" model=<path-to-quantized-model.pt> nndct_quant=True quant_mode=test batch=1 imgsz=800 dump_xmodel=True dump_onnx=True --nndct_convert_sigmoid_to_hsigmoid --nndct_convert_silu_to_hswish
60	    ```
61	
62	  * **Evaluate Performance**: To test the quantized model's accuracy without re-exporting, run:
63	
64	    ```bash
65	    yolo detect val data="xview3-vitis.yaml" model=<path-to-quantized-model.pt> nndct_quant=True quant_mode=test imgsz=800 dump_xmodel=False dump_onnx=False --nndct_convert_sigmoid_to_hsigmoid --nndct_convert_silu_to_hswish
66	    ```
67	
68	-----
69	
70	### 3\. Compiling the Model for the DPU
71	
72	The exported `xmodel` must be compiled for the specific DPU (Deep Learning Processing Unit) architecture on your target hardware.
73	
74	  * **Compile with `vai_c_xir`**:
75	
76	    ```bash
77	    vai_c_xir -x nndct_quant/DetectionModel_0_int.xmodel \
78	              -a /opt/vitis_ai/compiler/arch/DPUCZDX8G/KV260/arch.json \
79	              -o vai_c_output \
80	              -n my_yolov8_model
81	    ```
82	
83	    This command generates a directory (`vai_c_output`) containing the compiled model (`.xmodel`), a `meta.json` file, and a checksum. For more details, refer to the [Vitis AI Documentation](https://xilinx.github.io/Vitis-AI/3.5/html/docs/quickstart/vek280.html).
84	
85	-----
86	
87	### 4\. Preparing for Inference
88	
89	Before deploying, you need to create a model configuration file and identify the output layers.
90	
91	  * **Create a `prototxt` Configuration File**: Create a `model.prototxt` file with the same name as your compiled `.xmodel`. This file specifies post-processing parameters. See the [example file](https://www.google.com/search?q=./examples/YOLOv8-Vitis-AI/model.prototxt) for reference.
92	
93	  * **Identify Detection Layer Names**: Use the `xir` tool to find the output tensor names for your model:
94	
95	    ```bash
96	    xir subgraph yolov8.xmodel | grep DPU
97	    ```
98	
99	    Copy the tensor names from the output list (marked with `O`) into the `detect_layer_name` fields in your `.prototxt` file.
100	
101	  * **Deploy to Target Hardware**: Transfer the compiled model directory to your KV260:
102

# YOLOv8 Vitis AI Quantization

This directory contains the code for post-training quantization and quantization aware training of the YOLOv8 model using Vitis AI 3.5.

Due to the license restrictions of the original YOLOv8 repository, the code is not provided instead an overview of modifications is provided [here](modifications.md). Please see the [YOLOv7 Vitis AI implementation](https://github.com/Xilinx/Vitis-AI-Copyleft-Model-Zoo/tree/main/yolov7) for a similar implementation, or contact AMD for additional information.

## Installation

Clone the modified `ultralytics` repo (referred to as `ultralytics-vitis-ai`) in your Vitis AI file system and install the library as an editable package in the running Vitis AI Docker container using `pip` with the following command:
```bash
cd ultralytics-vitis-ai/
conda activate vitis-ai-pytorch
pip install -e .
```

>[!WARNING]
>Ensure that the Python package installation path is included in your system's `PATH` environment variable.
>```bash
>export PATH="/home/vitis-ai-user/.local/bin:$PATH"
>```
>The YOLOv8 requirements clash with the `pandas` and `matplotlib` libraries included in the Vitis AI conda environment, which can be resolved by running:
>```bash
>pip install pandas --no-cache-dir --force-reinstall && pip install matplotlib --no-cache-dir --force-reinstall
>```

## Vitis AI Quantization Steps 

This guide outlines the process for quantizing, compiling, and deploying a trained YOLOv8 model to a Xilinx Kria KV260 using the Vitis AI toolchain.


### 1\. Model Quantization

Model quantization reduces the precision of the model's weights and activations, significantly decreasing its size and improving inference speed on hardware accelerators. You can choose between Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) or Quantization Aware Training (QAT).

#### Post-Training Quantization (PTQ)

PTQ is a quick method to quantize an already trained model. First, calibrate the model to determine the dynamic range of activations:

```bash
yolo detect val data="xview3-vitis.yaml" model=<path-to-your-model.pt> nndct_quant=True quant_mode=calib imgsz=800 --nndct_convert_sigmoid_to_hsigmoid --nndct_convert_silu_to_hswish
```

#### Quantization Aware Training (QAT)

QAT can yield better performance by simulating quantization effects during the training process, allowing the model to adapt.

```bash
yolo detect train cfg="qat.yaml" data="xview3-vitis.yaml" model=<path-to-your-model.pt> imgsz=800 epochs=100 optimizer=SGD momentum=0.9 lr0=0.005 warmup_epochs=0 nndct_quant=True --nndct_convert_sigmoid_to_hsigmoid --nndct_convert_silu_to_hswish
```
-----

### 2\. Model Export and Evaluation

Once the model is quantized, you can export it to the `xmodel` format for FPGA deployment and the `ONNX` format for broader compatibility.

  * **Export the Quantized Model**:

    ```bash
    yolo detect val data="xview3-vitis.yaml" model=<path-to-quantized-model.pt> nndct_quant=True quant_mode=test batch=1 imgsz=800 dump_xmodel=True dump_onnx=True --nndct_convert_sigmoid_to_hsigmoid --nndct_convert_silu_to_hswish
    ```

  * **Evaluate Performance**: To test the quantized model's accuracy without re-exporting, run:

    ```bash
    yolo detect val data="xview3-vitis.yaml" model=<path-to-quantized-model.pt> nndct_quant=True quant_mode=test imgsz=800 dump_xmodel=False dump_onnx=False --nndct_convert_sigmoid_to_hsigmoid --nndct_convert_silu_to_hswish
    ```

-----

### 3\. Compiling the Model for the DPU

The exported `xmodel` must be compiled for the specific DPU (Deep Learning Processing Unit) architecture on your target hardware.

  * **Compile with `vai_c_xir`**:

    ```bash
    vai_c_xir -x nndct_quant/DetectionModel_0_int.xmodel \
              -a /opt/vitis_ai/compiler/arch/DPUCZDX8G/KV260/arch.json \
              -o vai_c_output \
              -n my_yolov8_model
    ```

    This command generates a directory (`vai_c_output`) containing the compiled model (`.xmodel`), a `meta.json` file, and a checksum. For more details, refer to the [Vitis AI Documentation](https://xilinx.github.io/Vitis-AI/3.5/html/docs/quickstart/vek280.html).

-----

### 4\. Preparing for Inference

Before deploying, you need to create a model configuration file and identify the output layers.

  * **Create a `prototxt` Configuration File**: Create a `model.prototxt` file with the same name as your compiled `.xmodel`. This file specifies post-processing parameters. See the [example file](https://www.google.com/search?q=./examples/YOLOv8-Vitis-AI/model.prototxt) for reference.

  * **Identify Detection Layer Names**: Use the `xir` tool to find the output tensor names for your model:

    ```bash
    xir subgraph yolov8.xmodel | grep DPU
    ```

    Copy the tensor names from the output list (marked with `O`) into the `detect_layer_name` fields in your `.prototxt` file.

  * **Deploy to Target Hardware**: Transfer the compiled model directory to your KV260:

    ```bash
    scp -r model/ <target-user>@<target-IP-address>:~/usr/share/vitis_ai_library/models/
    ```

1	# On-Board Inference
2	
3	## Setup
4	This repository contains the code for running inference on the Kria KV260 MPSoC using the Vitis AI framework with our patch (`/framework/vitis_ai`). The inference application is designed to evaluate the performance of YOLOv8 models trained on the xView3-SAR dataset.
5	
6	```bash
7	sh build.sh
8	```
9	
10	In order to run the inference application, a list of test images in a text file (`test-image-list.txt`) is required. Each line in this file should contain the path to an image in a tiff format, of input size 800x800 pixels.
11	
12	## Usage
13	The inference application provides two main functionalities: model accuracy benchmarking and model throughput performance evaluation.
14	1.  **Model Accuracy Benchmarking:** To evaluate the accuracy of the model on a test dataset, you can run:
15	    ```bash
16	    PIOU2_NMS=<0|1> ./xview3_benchmark <yolov8-model-name> <test-image-list.txt> <output-file.txt> -t <number-of-threads>
17	    ```
18	    Setting `PIOU2_NMS` to `1` will use the PIoU2 of box IoU calculation, otherwise will use the default CIoU. The script will output the predictions in a txt file (`output-file.txt`) in the following format `chip_id,label,x,y,w,h,score`, which can be read as a csv. 
19	
20	2.  **Model Throughput Performance:** To evaluate the throughput of the model, you can run:
21	    ```bash
22	    DEEPHI_PROFILING=<0|1> ./xview3_performance <yolov8-model-name> <test-image-list.txt> -t <number-of-threads>
23	    ```
24	    The script will output the FPS (Frames Per Second) and the average time taken for each inference run.
25	    Enabling `DEEPHI_PROFILING` will output the time taken for the pre-processing, DPU inference and post-processing steps. When profiling is enabled, we recommend piping the output to a file for easier analysis. 
26	
27	
28	![Inference Breakdown](../../assets/inference_breakdown.jpg)
29	<sub>Processing time breakdown of YOLOv8 variants, each bar represents average time from 100 consecutive inference runs.</sub>

# On-Board Inference

## Setup
This repository contains the code for running inference on the Kria KV260 MPSoC using the Vitis AI framework with our patch (`/framework/vitis_ai`). The inference application is designed to evaluate the performance of YOLOv8 models trained on the xView3-SAR dataset.

```bash
sh build.sh
```

In order to run the inference application, a list of test images in a text file (`test-image-list.txt`) is required. Each line in this file should contain the path to an image in a tiff format, of input size 800x800 pixels.

## Usage
The inference application provides two main functionalities: model accuracy benchmarking and model throughput performance evaluation.
1.  **Model Accuracy Benchmarking:** To evaluate the accuracy of the model on a test dataset, you can run:
    ```bash
    PIOU2_NMS=<0|1> ./xview3_benchmark <yolov8-model-name> <test-image-list.txt> <output-file.txt> -t <number-of-threads>
    ```
    Setting `PIOU2_NMS` to `1` will use the PIoU2 of box IoU calculation, otherwise will use the default CIoU. The script will output the predictions in a txt file (`output-file.txt`) in the following format `chip_id,label,x,y,w,h,score`, which can be read as a csv. 

2.  **Model Throughput Performance:** To evaluate the throughput of the model, you can run:
    ```bash
    DEEPHI_PROFILING=<0|1> ./xview3_performance <yolov8-model-name> <test-image-list.txt> -t <number-of-threads>
    ```
    The script will output the FPS (Frames Per Second) and the average time taken for each inference run.
    Enabling `DEEPHI_PROFILING` will output the time taken for the pre-processing, DPU inference and post-processing steps. When profiling is enabled, we recommend piping the output to a file for easier analysis. 


![Inference Breakdown](../../assets/inference_breakdown.jpg)
<sub>Processing time breakdown of YOLOv8 variants, each bar represents average time from 100 consecutive inference runs.</sub>

# Dataset

Download the dataset on the [xView3-SAR](https://iuu.xview.us) competition webpage using the `aria2` tool, and extract the scenes using: 
```bash
for file in *.tar.gz; do tar xzvf "${file}" && rm "${file}"; done
```
The dataset should be structured in a directory as such:
```            
├── data               <- SAR .tiff scenes
│   ├── training
|   │   ├── e98ca5aba8849b06t
|   │   └── ...
│   ├── validation
|   │   ├── b1844cde847a3942v
|   │   └── ...
│   └── public
|       ├── 04584ce7faec545dp
|       └── ...
│
├── labels             <- Detection .csv annotations
│   ├── training.csv
│   ├── validation.csv
│   └── public.csv
│
└── shoreline          <- Shoreline .npy coordinates
    ├── training
    │   ├── e98ca5aba8849b06t_shoreline.npy
    │   └── ...
    ├── validation
    │   ├── b1844cde847a3942v_shoreline.npy
    │   └── ...
    └── public
        ├── 04584ce7faec545dp_shoreline.npy
        └── ...
```
YOLOv8 requires cropped chips and associated labels in individual `.txt` files for training, evaluation and inference. Pre-processing of the dataset can be done using: 
```bash
python generate_xview3.py --labels <path-to-labels> --source <path-to-data> --save_dir <destination> --imgsz <800|640> --name <name-of-the-dataset>
```

import argparse
import os
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import random
import rasterio
import warnings

from rasterio.enums import Resampling
from rasterio.errors import NotGeoreferencedWarning
from tqdm import tqdm

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=NotGeoreferencedWarning)

NODATA = -32768
WIDTH_MEDIAN = 0.01
HEIGHT_MEDIAN = 0.01


def get_annots(
    out: np.ndarray, row_start: int, col_start: int, imgsz: int, nan_true: bool = False
) -> np.ndarray:
    """ Transform annotations to YOLO format. """
    if nan_true:
        is_vessel_vec = np.asarray(out[:, 2] == True).astype(int)
        is_fishing_vec = np.asarray(
            (out[:, 3] == True) | ((np.isnan(out[:, 3])) & (out[:, 2] == True))
        ).astype(int)
    else:
        is_vessel_vec = np.asarray(out[:, 2] == True).astype(int)
        is_fishing_vec = np.asarray(out[:, 3] == True).astype(int)
    labels = is_vessel_vec + is_fishing_vec

    x = out[:, 0] - row_start
    y = out[:, 1] - col_start

    width = np.ones_like(x) * WIDTH_MEDIAN
    height = np.ones_like(y) * HEIGHT_MEDIAN
    x = x / imgsz
    y = y / imgsz

    return np.stack([labels, y, x, width, height], axis=1)


def main(labels: str, source: str, save_dir: str, imgsz: int, name: str):
    """ Generate chips from xView3 dataset. """
    NODATA = -32768

    df = pd.read_csv(labels)
    df.is_fishing = df.is_fishing.astype(float)
    df.is_vessel = df.is_vessel.astype(float)
    scenes = os.listdir(source)

    train_counter = 0
    pos_idx, pos_scene_id, pos_row_start, pos_col_start = [], [], [], []
    neg_idx, neg_scene_id, neg_row_start, neg_col_start = [], [], [], []

    # create directories if they don't exist
    directories = [f"images/{name}", f"labels/{name}", f"labels/{name}_kp"]    
    for directory in directories:
        os.makedirs(os.path.join(save_dir, directory), exist_ok=True)

    # Generate all crop coordinates
    for scene in tqdm(scenes):
        with rasterio.open(os.path.join(source, scene, "VH_dB.tif")) as src:
            vh = src.read(1)
        with rasterio.open(os.path.join(source, scene, "VV_dB.tif")) as src:
            vv = src.read(1)
        with rasterio.open(os.path.join(source, scene, "bathymetry.tif")) as src:
            bathymetry = src.read(
                out_shape=(
                    vh.shape[0],
                    vh.shape[1],
                ),
                resampling=Resampling.bilinear,
            ).squeeze()

        # Get scene data
        out_dtype = vh.dtype
        scene_size = vh.shape
        scene_df = df[df["scene_id"] == scene]

        # Generate all crop coordinates
        rows = np.arange(0, scene_size[0] - imgsz + 1, imgsz)
        cols = np.arange(0, scene_size[1] - imgsz + 1, imgsz)
        row_starts, col_starts = np.meshgrid(rows, cols)

        # Create views for all crops
        crops = np.lib.stride_tricks.sliding_window_view(vh, (imgsz, imgsz))[
            row_starts.flatten(), col_starts.flatten()
        ]

        # Find NODATA chips
        valid_mask = ~np.all(crops == NODATA, axis=(1, 2))
        del crops
        valid_row_starts = row_starts.flatten()[valid_mask]
        valid_col_starts = col_starts.flatten()[valid_mask]
        scene_neg_idx, scene_neg_scene_id, scene_neg_row_start, scene_neg_col_start = (
            [],
            [],
            [],
            [],
        )
        scene_chips = 0

        for idx, (row_start, col_start) in enumerate(
            zip(valid_row_starts, valid_col_starts)
        ):
            out = scene_df.loc[
                (scene_df.detect_scene_row >= row_start)
                & (scene_df.detect_scene_row < row_start + imgsz)
                & (scene_df.detect_scene_column >= col_start)
                & (scene_df.detect_scene_column < col_start + imgsz),
                ["detect_scene_row", "detect_scene_column", "is_vessel", "is_fishing"],
            ]

            background = len(out) == 0

            if background:
                scene_neg_idx.append(str(train_counter).zfill(8))
                scene_neg_scene_id.append(scene)
                scene_neg_row_start.append(row_start)
                scene_neg_col_start.append(col_start)
            else:
                vv_crop = vv[
                    row_start : row_start + imgsz, col_start : col_start + imgsz
                ]
                vh_crop = vh[
                    row_start : row_start + imgsz, col_start : col_start + imgsz
                ]
                bathymetry_crop = bathymetry[
                    row_start : row_start + imgsz, col_start : col_start + imgsz
                ]
                with rasterio.open(
                    os.path.join(
                        save_dir,
                        "images",
                        name,
                        f"{str(train_counter).zfill(8)}.tif",
                    ),
                    "w",
                    driver="GTiff",
                    height=imgsz,
                    width=imgsz,
                    count=3,
                    dtype=out_dtype,
                ) as dst:
                    dst.write(vv_crop, 1)
                    dst.write(vh_crop, 2)
                    dst.write(bathymetry_crop, 3)

                scene_chips += 1
                pos_idx.append(str(train_counter).zfill(8))
                pos_scene_id.append(scene)
                pos_row_start.append(row_start)
                pos_col_start.append(col_start)

                # With relaxed annotations
                annots = get_annots(out.values, row_start, col_start, imgsz, True)
                with open(
                    os.path.join(
                        save_dir,
                        "labels",
                        name,
                        f"{str(train_counter).zfill(8)}.txt",
                    ),
                    "w",
                ) as f:
                    for annot in annots:
                        f.write(
                            " ".join([str(int(annot[0]))] + [str(a) for a in annot[1:]])
                            + "\n"
                        )
                annots = np.hstack((annots, annots[:, 1:3]))
                with open(
                    os.path.join(
                        save_dir,
                        "labels",
                        f"{name}_kp",
                        f"{str(train_counter).zfill(8)}.txt",
                    ),
                    "w",
                ) as f:
                    for annot in annots:
                        f.write(
                            " ".join([str(int(annot[0]))] + [str(a) for a in annot[1:]])
                            + "\n"
                        )

            train_counter += 1

        # Get 30% background chips
        n_scene_background = int(scene_chips * 0.3)
        n_all_scene_background = len(scene_neg_idx)
        idx_scene_background = random.sample(
            range(n_all_scene_background), n_scene_background
        )

        for idx in idx_scene_background:
            chip_idx = scene_neg_idx[idx]
            chip_scene_id = scene_neg_scene_id[idx]
            row_start = scene_neg_row_start[idx]
            col_start = scene_neg_col_start[idx]
            neg_idx.append(chip_idx)
            neg_scene_id.append(chip_scene_id)
            neg_row_start.append(row_start)
            neg_col_start.append(col_start)

            vv_crop = vv[row_start : row_start + imgsz, col_start : col_start + imgsz]
            vh_crop = vh[row_start : row_start + imgsz, col_start : col_start + imgsz]
            bathymetry_crop = bathymetry[
                row_start : row_start + imgsz, col_start : col_start + imgsz
            ]

            with rasterio.open(
                os.path.join(
                    save_dir, "images", name, f"{str(chip_idx).zfill(8)}.tif"
                ),
                "w",
                driver="GTiff",
                height=imgsz,
                width=imgsz,
                count=3,
                dtype=out_dtype,
            ) as dst:
                dst.write(vv_crop, 1)
                dst.write(vh_crop, 2)
                dst.write(bathymetry_crop, 3)

    # Save the coordinates of each chip
    pos_out_df = pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "idx": pos_idx,
            "scene_id": pos_scene_id,
            "row_start": pos_row_start,
            "col_start": pos_col_start,
        }
    )
    pos_out_df.to_csv(
        os.path.join(save_dir, f"{name}_positive_coords.txt"),
        header=False,
        index=False,
        sep=" ",
    )
    neg_out_df = pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "idx": neg_idx,
            "scene_id": neg_scene_id,
            "row_start": neg_row_start,
            "col_start": neg_col_start,
        }
    )
    neg_out_df.to_csv(
        os.path.join(save_dir, f"{name}_negative_coords.txt"),
        header=False,
        index=False,
        sep=" ",
    )


if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--labels", type=str, help="Path to the labels file")
    parser.add_argument("--source", type=str, help="Path to the source directory")
    parser.add_argument("--save_dir", type=str, help="Path to the save directory")
    parser.add_argument("--imgsz", type=int, help="Size of the image", default=800)
    parser.add_argument("--name", type=str, help="Name of the dataset")

    args = parser.parse_args()
    main(args.labels, args.source, args.save_dir, args.imgsz, args.name)

# YOLOv8 Vitis AI Quantization Modifications

1. **`task.py`:**
    - In `__init__` and `forward`:
        - Initialize quant and dequant stubs from `pytorch_nndct.nn`
        - Add a quant argument to forward function and call stubs before and after model predict

    - In `attempt_load`:
        - If QAT, add loading of qat_ema_state_dict into model from checkpoint and convert the quantized model to a deployable model for evaluation
        - If PTQ write qat_ema_quant_info from checkpoint to a quant_info.json file



2. **`trainer.py`:**
    - In `_setup_train`:
        - Create `ori_model` and `ori_model_ema` class attributes as deep copies of `self.model`
        - Override pytorch `__deepcopy__` operator to patch error with custom tensor subclass
        - Create `QatProcessor`, with bitwidth of 8
        - Define `self.model = self.qat_processor.trainable_model(calib_dir=calib_dir.as_posix())`

    - After each epoch before validation:
        - Create deployable model with `self.qat_processor.convert_to_deployable` from model and model EMA in `qat/deployableema|deployable`
        - Update `ori_model` and `ori_model_ema` with `state_dict`

    - In `save_model`:
        - Add `qat_model_quant_info`, `qat_model_quant_info_test`, `qat_model_state_dict` and `qat_ema_state_dict`
        - Get quant info from previous `convert_to_deployable` call

    - In `build_optimizer`: 
        - Select model parameters with "threshold" in the name 
        - Create a parameter group for the QAT thresholds with a learning rate ~100x larger than one used for model and add to `optimizer`

3. **`modules.py`:**
    - Modifications to `C2f`, `SPPF`, `Detect` head

    - Modify `torch.cat` to `pytorch_nndct.nn.modules.functional.cat`
    - Initializing a cat object for each operation instead of reusing

    - Initialize `nn.MaxPool2d` objects for each operation instead of reusing

4. **`validator.py`:**
    - In `__call__`:
        - Create a `torch_quantizer` object, passing the quant mode test/calib parameter, model, output directory and bit width 
        - Run inference on `quantizer.quant_model`

        - For calibration, add `quantizer.export_quant_config()`

        - For xmodel/ONNX exporting add
            ```python

            quantizer.export_onnx_model(output_dir=output_dir.as_posix(), verbose=True, dynamic_batch=True)
            ```

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from scipy.spatial import cKDTree


def nms(pred: pd.DataFrame, distance_thresh: int = 10):
    """
    Prune detections that are redundant due to a nearby higher-scoring detection.

    Args:
        pred (pd.DataFrame): dataframe containing detections, from inference.py
        distance_threshold (int): if points are within this threshold, only keep higher score point
    """
    elim_inds = set()

    for scene_id in pred.scene_id.unique():
        # Get a DataFrame for the current scene.
        scene_df = pred[pred.scene_id == scene_id]

        # If there's only one detection, there's nothing to suppress.
        if len(scene_df) < 2:
            continue

        coords = scene_df[['detect_scene_row', 'detect_scene_column']].values
        scores = scene_df['score'].values
        # Keep track of original DataFrame indices.
        original_indices = scene_df.index.values

        kdtree = cKDTree(coords)
        order = np.lexsort((-original_indices, scores))[::-1]
        is_eliminated = np.zeros(len(scene_df), dtype=bool)

        for i in range(len(scene_df)):
            idx_in_scene = order[i]

            # If this detection has already been suppressed by a higher-scoring one, skip.
            if is_eliminated[idx_in_scene]:
                continue

            # Find neighbors to suppress - query_ball_point finds all points within the given radius
            neighbors_indices = kdtree.query_ball_point(coords[idx_in_scene], r=distance_thresh)

            for neighbor_idx in neighbors_indices:
                if neighbor_idx != idx_in_scene:
                    is_eliminated[neighbor_idx] = True

        eliminated_in_scene = original_indices[is_eliminated]
        elim_inds.update(eliminated_in_scene)

    print("nms: drop {} of {}".format(len(elim_inds), len(pred)))

    return pred.drop(list(elim_inds))

101	    # Check matched pairs came from "LOW" labels
102	    for pair in low_inds:
103	        assert (
104	            gt.iloc[pair["gt_idx"]]["confidence"] == "LOW"
105	        ), f"Index {pair['gt_idx']} is {gt.iloc[pair['gt_idx']]['confidence']}"
106	
107	    low_pred_inds = [a["pred_idx"] for a in low_inds]
108	
109	    df_out = pred.drop(index=low_pred_inds)
110	    df_out = df_out.reset_index()
111	    return df_out

114	def match_low_confidence_preds(preds, gt, distance_tolerance=200, costly_dist=False):
115	    """
116	    Matches detections in a predictions dataframe to a ground truth data frame and isolate the low confidence matches
117	
118	    Args:
119	        preds (pd.DataFrame): contains inference results for a
120	            single scene
121	        gt (pd.DataFrame): contains ground truth labels for a single
122	            scene
123	        distance_tolerance (int, optional): Maximum distance
124	            for valid detection. Defaults to 200.
125	        costly_dist (bool): whether to assign 9999999 to entries in the
126	            distance metrics greater than distance_tolerance; defaults to False
127	
128	    Returns:
129	        low_inds (list, int): list of indices for the preds dataframe that are
130	            associated as (1) correct detection in the *global* preds dataframe; (2) low confidence in the corresponding gt dataframe
131	    """
132	
133	    # Getting pixel-level predicted and ground-truth detections
134	    pred_array = np.array(
135	        list(zip(preds["detect_scene_row"], preds["detect_scene_column"]))
136	    )
137	    gt_array = np.array(list(zip(gt["detect_scene_row"], gt["detect_scene_column"])))
138	
139	    # Getting a list of index with LOW in the ground truth dataframe
140	    low_gt_inds = list(gt[gt["confidence"] == "LOW"].index)
141	
142	    # Building distance matrix using Euclidean distance pixel space
143	    # multiplied by the UTM resolution (10 m per pixel)
144	    dist_mat = distance_matrix(pred_array, gt_array, p=2) * PIX_TO_M
145	    if costly_dist:
146	        dist_mat[dist_mat > distance_tolerance] = 9999999 * PIX_TO_M
147	
148	    # Using Hungarian matching algorithm to assign lowest-cost gt-pred pairs
149	    rows, cols = linear_sum_assignment(dist_mat)
150	
151	    low_inds = [
152	        {"pred_idx": preds.index[rows[ii]], "gt_idx": gt.index[cols[ii]]}
153	        for ii in range(len(rows))
154	        if (dist_mat[rows[ii], cols[ii]] < distance_tolerance)
155	        and (gt.index[cols[ii]] in low_gt_inds)
156	    ]
157	
158	    return low_inds

161	def get_shore_preds(df, shoreline_root, scene_id, shore_tolerance_km):
162	    """
163	    Getting detections that are close to the shoreline
164	
165	    Args:
166	        df (pd.DataFrame): dataframe containing detections
167	        shoreline_root (str): path to shoreline contour files
168	        scene_id (str): scene_id
169	        shore_tolerance_km (float): "close to shore" tolerance in km
170	
171	    Returns:
172	        df_close (pd.DataFrame): subset of df containing only detections close to shore
173	    """
174	    # Loading shoreline contours for distance-to-shore calculation
175	    shoreline_contours = np.load(
176	        f"{shoreline_root}/{scene_id}_shoreline.npy", allow_pickle=True
177	    )
178	
179	    # If there are no shorelines in the scene
180	    if len(shoreline_contours) == 0:
181	        return pd.DataFrame()
182	
183	    contour_points = np.vstack(shoreline_contours)
184	
185	    # Creating KD trees and computing distance matrix
186	    tree1 = KDTree(np.array(contour_points))
187	    tree2 = KDTree(
188	        np.array([df["detect_scene_row"], df["detect_scene_column"]]).transpose()
189	    )
190	    sdm = tree1.sparse_distance_matrix(tree2, shore_tolerance_km * 1000 / PIX_TO_M, p=2)
191	    dists = sdm.toarray()
192	
193	    # Make it so we can use np.min() to find smallest distance b/t each detection and any contour point
194	    dists[dists == 0] = 9999999
195	    min_shore_dists = np.min(dists, axis=0)
196	    close_shore_inds = np.where(min_shore_dists != 9999999)
197	    df_close = df.iloc[close_shore_inds]
198	    return df_close

201	def compute_loc_performance(preds, gt, distance_tolerance=200, costly_dist=False):
202	    """
203	    Computes maritime object detection performance from a prediction
204	    dataframe and a ground truth datafr
205	
206	    Args:
207	        preds (pd.DataFrame): contains inference results for a
208	            single scene
209	        gt (pd.DataFrame): contains ground truth labels for a single
210	            scene
211	        distance_tolerance (int, optional): Maximum distance
212	            for valid detection. Defaults to 200.
213	        costly_dist (bool): whether to assign 9999999 to entries in the distance metrics greater than distance_tolerance; defaults to False
214	
215	    Returns:
216	        tp_ind (list, dict): list of dicts with keys 'pred_idx', 'gt_idx';
217	            values for each are the indices preds and gt that are
218	            associated as correct detection in the *global* preds and
219	            gt dataframes
220	        fp_ind (list): list of indices in the *global* preds dataframe
221	            that are not assigned to a gt detection by the matching
222	        fn_ind (list): list of indices in the *global* gt dataframe
223	            that do not match any detection in pred within dist_tol
224	    """
225	
226	    # Getting pixel-level predicted and ground-truth detections
227	    pred_array = np.array(
228	        list(zip(preds["detect_scene_row"], preds["detect_scene_column"]))
229	    )
230	    gt_array = np.array(list(zip(gt["detect_scene_row"], gt["detect_scene_column"])))
231	
232	    # Building distance matrix using Euclidean distance pixel space
233	    # multiplied by the UTM resolution (10 m per pixel)
234	    dist_mat = distance_matrix(pred_array, gt_array, p=2) * PIX_TO_M
235	    if costly_dist:
236	        dist_mat[dist_mat > distance_tolerance] = 9999999 * PIX_TO_M
237	
238	    # Using Hungarian matching algorithm to assign lowest-cost gt-pred pairs
239	    rows, cols = linear_sum_assignment(dist_mat)
240	
241	    # Recording indices for tp, fp, fn
242	    tp_inds = [
243	        {"pred_idx": preds.index[rows[ii]], "gt_idx": gt.index[cols[ii]]}
244	        for ii in range(len(rows))
245	        if dist_mat[rows[ii], cols[ii]] < distance_tolerance
246	    ]
247	    tp_pred_inds = [a["pred_idx"] for a in tp_inds]
248	    tp_gt_inds = [a["gt_idx"] for a in tp_inds]
249	
250	    fp_inds = [a for a in preds.index if a not in tp_pred_inds]
251	    fn_inds = [a for a in gt.index if a not in tp_gt_inds]
252	
253	    # Making sure each GT is associated with one true positive
254	    # or is in the false negative bin
255	    assert len(gt) == len(fn_inds) + len(tp_inds)
256	
257	    return tp_inds, fp_inds, fn_inds
258

275	            model output both have a False 'is_vessel' label
276	        c_fp_inds: list of dicts from tp_inds where ground truth

284	    c_tn_inds = []
285	    # For every box where you have a matching detection, do the labels match?
286	    for pair in tp_inds:
287	        # Making sure we only use valid GTs
288	        if isinstance(gt[pair["gt_idx"]], float):
289	            if np.isnan(gt[pair["gt_idx"]]):
290	                continue

354	def compute_length_performance(preds, gt, tp_inds):
355	    """
356	    Compute aggregate percent error for vessel size estimation
357	
358	    Args:
359	        preds ([pd.Dataframe)): contains inference results for all scenes
360	        gt (pd.Dataframe): contains ground truth labels for all scenes
361	        tp_inds (list, dict): List of dicts output from compute_loc_performance
362	            containing indices of true positive detection pairs in the global preds
363	            and gt dataframes
364	
365	    Returns:
366	        length_performance (float): aggregate percent error for vessel length estimation
367	    """
368	    pct_error = 0.0
369	    num_valid_gt = 0.0
370	
371	    for pair in tp_inds:
372	        if isinstance(gt[pair["gt_idx"]], float):
373	            if np.isnan(gt[pair["gt_idx"]]):
374	                continue
375	        # The longest vessel in the world was 458m, and fixed-infrastructure is smaller.
376	        # To bound the maximum possible error, we cap preds and ground truth
377	        # at MAX_OBJECT_LENGTH_M, which by default is 500m
378	        gt_pred = min(gt[pair["gt_idx"]], MAX_OBJECT_LENGTH_M)
379	        inf_pred = min(preds[pair["pred_idx"]], MAX_OBJECT_LENGTH_M)
380	        pct_error += np.abs(inf_pred - gt_pred) / gt_pred
381	        num_valid_gt += 1
382	
383	    if num_valid_gt == 0:
384	        length_performance = 0
385	    else:
386	        length_performance = 1.0 - min((pct_error / num_valid_gt), 1.0)
387	
388	    return length_performance

391	def calculate_p_r_f(num_tp, num_fp, num_fn):
392	    """
393	    Compute precision, recall, and F1 score
394	
395	    Args:
396	        tp_inds (list, dict): list of dicts with keys 'pred_idx', 'gt_idx';
397	            values for each are the indices preds and gt that are
398	            associated as correct detection in the *global* preds and
399	            gt dataframes
400	        fp_inds (list): list of indices in the *global* preds dataframe
401	            that are not assigned to a gt detection by the matching
402	        fn_inds (list): list of indices in the *global* gt dataframe
403	            that do not match any detection in pred within dist_tol
404	
405	    Returns:
406	        precision (float): precision score
407	        recall (float): recall score
408	        fscore (float): f1 score
409	    """
410	    try:
411	        precision = num_tp / (num_tp + num_fp)
412	    except ZeroDivisionError:
413	        precision = 0
414	    try:
415	        recall = num_tp / (num_tp + num_fn)
416	    except ZeroDivisionError:
417	        recall = 0
418	    try:
419	        fscore = (2 * precision * recall) / (precision + recall)
420	    except ZeroDivisionError:
421	        fscore = 0
422	
423	    if precision == np.nan or recall == np.nan or fscore == np.nan:
424	        return 0, 0, 0
425	    else:
426	        return precision, recall, fscore

466	def aggregate_f(
467	    loc_fscore, length_acc, vessel_fscore, fishing_fscore, loc_fscore_shore
468	):
469	    """
470	    Compute aggregate metric for xView3 scoring
471	
472	
473	    Args:
474	        loc_fscore (float): F1 score for overall maritime object detection
475	        length_acc (float): Aggregate percent error for vessel length estimation
476	        vessel_fscore (float): F1 score for vessel vs. non-vessel task
477	        fishing_fscore (float): F1 score for fishing vessel vs. non-fishing vessel task
478	        loc_fscore_shore (float): F1 score for close-to-shore maritime object detection
479	
480	    Returns:
481	        aggregate (float): aggregate metric for xView3 scoring
482	    """
483	
484	    # Note: should be between zero and one, and score should be heavily weighted on
485	    # overall maritime object detection!
486	    aggregate = (
487	        loc_fscore
488	        * (1 + length_acc + vessel_fscore + fishing_fscore + loc_fscore_shore)
489	        / 5
490	    )
491	
492	    return aggregate

588	def score(
589	    pred: pd.DataFrame,
590	    gt: pd.DataFrame,
591	    shore_root: str,
592	    model_name: str,
593	    epoch: int,
594	    num_candidates: int,
595	    conf_threshold: float,
596	    distance_tolerance: int = 200,
597	    shore_tolerance: int = 2,
598	    costly_dist: bool = False,
599	    verbose: bool = True,
600	):
601	    """Compute xView3 aggregate score from
602	
603	    Args:
604	        pred ([pd.Dataframe)): contains inference results for all scenes
605	        gt (pd.Dataframe): contains ground truth labels for all scenes]
606	        shoreline_root (str): path to shoreline contour files
607	        distance_tolerance (float): Maximum distance
608	            for valid detection. Defaults to 200.
609	        shore_tolerance (float): "close to shore" tolerance in km; defaults to 2
610	        costly_dist (bool): whether to assign 9999999 to entries in the distance metrics greater than distance_tolerance; defaults to False
611	
612	    Returns:
613	        scores (dict): dictionary containing aggregate xView score and
614	            all constituent scores
615	    """
616	    scores = {}
617	
618	    tp_inds, fp_inds, fn_inds = [], [], []
619	
620	    # For each scene, obtain the tp, fp, and fn indices for maritime
621	    # object detection in the *global* pred and gt dataframes
622	    for scene_id in tqdm(gt["scene_id"].unique()):
623	        pred_sc = pred[pred["scene_id"] == scene_id]
624	        gt_sc = gt[gt["scene_id"] == scene_id]
625	        (
626	            tp_inds_sc,
627	            fp_inds_sc,
628	            fn_inds_sc,
629	        ) = compute_loc_performance(
630	            pred_sc,
631	            gt_sc,
632	            distance_tolerance=distance_tolerance,
633	            costly_dist=costly_dist,
634	        )
635	
636	        tp_inds += tp_inds_sc
637	        fp_inds += fp_inds_sc
638	        fn_inds += fn_inds_sc
639	
640	    # Compute precision, recall, and F1 for maritime object detection
641	    loc_precision, loc_recall, loc_fscore = calculate_p_r_f(tp_inds, fp_inds, fn_inds)
642	
643	    if verbose:
644	        verbose_evaluation(pred, gt, tp_inds, fp_inds, fn_inds)
645	
646	    # Allowing code to be run without shore data -- will output 0 for these scores
647	    if shore_tolerance and shore_root:
648	        # For each scene, compute distances to shore for model predictions, and isolate
649	        # both predictions and ground truth that are within the appropriate distance
650	        # from shore.  Note that for the predictions, we include any predictions within
